waves always came from producing tools and software for humans to optimize getting work done. Spreadsheets helped accountants and analysts, a conveyor belt increased throughput, and a","plain_text":"Productivity waves always came from producing tools and software for humans to optimize getting work done. Spreadsheets helped accountants and analysts, a conveyor belt increased throughput, and a hammer amplified leverage. But the actual labor always came from humans. 
AI is now producing work end to end performing the labor directly. It’s shipping code, closing support tickets and reviewing legal docs. There's a compression happening at the end of the stack. The old stack supported labor and the new stack produces it.
If you've listened to recent rhetoric around the financialization of AI you've probably heard Jensen and others saying llm tokens and/or GPU hours are the new commodity and the instinct makes sense because tokens are measurable, billable and easy to chart, and GPU hours have $B's flowing. But tokens are still just the meter, GPU hours are an input, and nobody wants either for their own sake. They want work done. AI turns the stack into a source of labor itself.
 
Machine Labor: economically useful work performed by software and sold into production.
The market is already moving this way. @benchmark's @sarahtavel's perspective is to map the opportunity through outsourced labor markets instead of software categories. If a focused offshore team or specialist services firm already exists to do a repeatable task, that task is often a good candidate for AI delivered work. @a16z's @arampell calls it software eating labor: software’s next act is doing the work itself. @sequoia's @JulienBek frames the same shift as services becoming software, where copilots sell tools but autopilots sell the work.
 
[https://sequoiacap.com/article/services-the-new-software/]
The Missing Market Behind Outcome Pricing
Seat pricing charges for access and token pricing charges for usage. Outcome pricing charges when work gets done. Outcome pricing gets you partly there but it still doesn’t tell you who sets the price.
If you can directly buy machine labor, prices should emerge from competition among suppliers that can satisfy the same task or job completion and that requires standardization across labor industries and the tasks within them.
The current way to do this is with llm tokens but raw tokens are the lowest layer. A barrel of oil is just a measurement unit. What trades is a barrel of a specific grade of oil, with known quality, delivery terms, and market price. A barrel of Brent is not the same as a barrel of heavy sour crude. LLM tokens work the same way. A token is just a measurement unit. What matters is the intelligence behind it: the model quality, benchmark floor, latency, context window, reliability, and delivery guarantees. One million tokens from a frontier coding model are not the same commodity as one million tokens from a cheap general purpose model. The market needs standardized inference grades, the same way energy markets need standardized oil grades.
@anjali_shriva makes this point directly: a token is not a fixed unit of cost. The economics vary with context length, task structure, input/output mix, retries, tool calls, and agentic workflows. A token in a short prompt and a token buried inside a long agent loop are not the same economic object.
We already do this with human labor. Nobody hires a radiologist as a generic human hour. They look at training, board certification, specialization, years of experience, availability, reputation, liability etc. Different human contract specs for different expectations of minimum standards and grades.
Human labor markets already run on specs that are messy, qualitative, and full of proxies. Machine Labor makes those specs much more explicit and measurable.
For an LLM or agent, the equivalent of skill, experience, speed, and reliability can be written directly into the contract: Benchmark score. Latency. Throughput. Context window. Max output length. Tool-use accuracy. Uptime. Error rate. We can source our labor with quantifiable expectations and outcomes.
TheGrid.ai contract spec is basically a qualification filter plus price competition for LLM output. A supplier qualifies if it clears the spec:
intelligence benchmark ≥ floor
latency ≤ max
throughput ≥ min
uptime ≥ min
error rate ≤ max
Once suppliers clear the same floor, they compete on price. The buyer is asking which supplier can deliver the required labor at the best price.
The radiology hire becomes measurable with LLMs that can read x-rays at high proficiency at a stated latency, context window and other explicit outcome based contract specs.
An outcome is how the buyer measures success, labor is the economic activity being supplied, and tokens are what the machine burns along the way.
The Grid is the market for Machine Labor.
From Tokens to Labor Markets for Machines
Markets can exist to price the inputs of the stack but in order to price the output, we need a market for machine labor. A buyer doesn’t care about GPU hours. And model endpoints are unstable too.They get renamed, deprecated, wrapped or retired.
Users and liquidity hate churn. GPUs and models evolve but the stable unit is the work.
Here’s how I see the market evolving. Each layer makes the thing being bought more abstract and more valuable but also harder to verify. The Grid should climb the ladder gradually.
Raw tokens → Commoditized LLM Capability Markets → Commoditized Labor Markets →  Programmable Outcome Markets
Phase 1
Raw Tokens: Claude 4.7, GPT 5.5, Kimi 2.6, DeepSeek V4, GLM 5 etc
Today buyers purchase raw model output from inference suppliers. They send their own prompts, receive inference and pay for usage. This is easy to verify, but it’s still raw. Buyers don’t really want tokens. They want useful intelligence at the best available price.
Phase 2
Commoditized LLM Capability Markets: text/usd, code/usd, agent/usd etc
Instead of picking a specific model, the buyer picks the class of intelligence they need. The buyer still owns the workflow, prompt, data and application logic. The Grid is just routing each request to the lowest priced qualifying model that meets the contract spec.
N.B. This is the first real abstraction above raw tokens and where TheGrid.ai sits today.
Phase 3
Commoditized Labor Markets: accounting/usd, support_agent/usd, legal/usd, healthcare/usd, radiology/usd etc
As models become more specialized, capability markets can become industry specific. This is similar to how humans specialize in different labor markets.
Here we sell inference that is well suited for workflows of that specific labor vertical. This will proliferate as niche industry models become more prevalent. Examples of this are Composer from @cursor_ai, @harvey  for legal work, @EvidenceOpen for healthcare.
Phase 4
Programmable RFQ and Outcome Markets for Agents: support_ticket_resolved/usd, pr_merged/usd, claim_processed/usd etc
The final layer is where The Grid starts moving from inference markets into Machine Labor markets.
This layer needs RFQs, escrow, delayed settlement, buyer confirmation, supplier reputation, clawbacks, dispute resolution and more. This likely starts with RFQs instead of an order book where a buyer defines the work, constraints, acceptance criteria, and settlement terms. Agents bid to complete it. The Grid helps route, price, verify, and settle the work.
This is the most valuable layer but the hardest to verify bc outcomes are delayed, subjective and gameable. A support ticket can reopen or a PR can pass tests but still create bad architecture.
All in price = cost to do the work + cost of bearing the risk
A workflow doesn’t become a market just because intelligence gets a market or becomes cheaper. Some work depends on private context like customer history or internal policies. The more context heavy the work, the less likely it is to clear cleanly in an open market. (@hypersoren https://hypersoren.xyz/posts/cybernetic-arbitrage/)
A market is needed to reveal which labor categories expand and which compress.
“Machine Labor vs Human Labor” or “Machine Labor & Human Labor”
@anjali_shriva's mechanism-design draft argues that the AI story is too often framed as replacement when in actuality it’s just coordination: how work, attribution, incentives, and value get reorganized when humans and machines both contribute to production. Today, a lot of AI usage inside firms is trapped because employees use AI privately, workflows stay locked with individuals, and firms cannot price or scale the productivity gains.
Most automatable work will likely move to machines. Some work becomes human review, accountability, training, and context management. In some cases, the last 1% of human judgment becomes more valuable because it unlocks the automated 99% at scale.
@rachelsupark's Brave New World of AI Markets says that the AI TAM should not be modeled as a simple replacement for existing human labor spend because it changes both price and quantity. Per-unit prices may fall as work gets cheaper to do, but quantity can expand because existing work gets consumed more often and entirely new work becomes economical. The piece frames this as P × Q (Market size = price per unit of work × quantity of work consumed)
If AI makes customer support interactions cheaper, companies can offer 24/7 capacity. The market doesn't become a cheaper version of the old support labor market. It can become a much larger market for customer interaction. 
AI is an expansion market because when the cost to do work gets cheaper the demand for it doesn’t stay fixed.
The Labor Layer
A market for machine labor should start where work can be spec’d clearly. GPU hours have too many inputs and only tell you what powered the work and pricing full outcomes is too messy and context heavy. As verification, reputation and risk/insurance pricing gets taken over by machines, then the market works towards pure outcomes.
Machine Labor can become tradable as buyers will care less about which model or gpu produced the work and more about if the work itself cleared the expected minimum standards and grades in the contract spec at the right price. Agents will care even less.
Machines can now perform economically useful work directly, and that work can be specified, measured, priced, sourced, and eventually traded. Electricity, compute, models, and tokens definitely still matter. But all of these still sit upstream.
Downstream is where work gets done and the market is moving toward something simpler: Machine Labor.

@The_GridAI ","title":"A Market for Machine Labor"}
